---
title: "The Grammar of Graphics Assembles"
subtitle: |
  [R]{.fragment fragment-index="2"}[, Python]{.fragment fragment-index="3"}[, SQL...]{.fragment fragment-index="4"} [and Now]{.fragment fragment-index="5"} [TYPST]{.cover-boom .fragment fragment-index="6"}
author:
  - name:
      given: "Mickaël"
      family: "CANOUIL"
    degrees: "Ph.D."
    url: "https://mickael.canouil.fr"
date: 2026-06-30
logo: assets/images/logo-stacked.svg
format:
  comic-revealjs:
    theme:
      - ./assets/stylesheets/custom.scss
    revealjs-plugins:
      - a11y
engine: markdown
filters:
  - typst-render
  - code-window
extensions:
  code-window:
    style: default
  typst-render:
    preamble: assets/typst/_preamble.typ
    format: svg
    align: center
    output-directory: ./assets/typst-render
    font-path: ./assets/fonts
    background: "#fdf6e3"
    foreground: "#0b0b0b"
    cache: false
---

# Issue 01: The Problem {.section}

Two runtimes, one document.

## Two Worlds Apart {.panel}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Typst + Gribouille**

```{.typst style="font-size: 0.9em;"}
#figure(
  plot(data: penguins, ...),
  caption: [Figure 1.],
)
```

One compiler.
One font system.
Document and figure share the same layout.

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**R / Python + import**

```r
ggplot(penguins) + ...
ggsave("fig.svg")
```

```typst
#image("fig.svg")
```

Two font systems.
Two colour palettes.
Two render passes.

:::
:::

::: {.narration}
Every figure is a tourist in its own document.
:::

## The Friction {.panel}

Working around the mismatch:

- [Different fonts between prose and figure.]{.fragment .comic-pop}
- [Different colour palettes — slide theme vs. ggplot2 theme.]{.fragment .comic-pop}
- [Two render passes — document and figure compiled separately.]{.fragment .comic-pop}
- [No shared layout — figure margins are guesswork.]{.fragment .comic-pop}

::: {.callout-warning .fragment collapse="false" title="The symptom"}
You fixed the font in the figure.
The document still uses a different one.
:::

# Issue 02: The Team {.section}

Same grammar, different runtimes.

## Grammar Travels {.panel}

::: {layout-ncol=2}

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="1"}
**ggplot2** `·` R

The original.
Wilkinson's grammar in idiomatic R.
:::

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="2"}
**plotnine** `·` Python

A faithful port.
The same `aes()` + layer vocabulary, now in Python.
:::

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="3"}
**ggsql** `·` SQL

Queries as layers.
The grammar applied when the runtime is a database.
:::

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="4"}
**Gribouille** `·` Typst

Native compilation.
The grammar runs inside the document engine itself.
:::

:::

{{< bam "R" colour=blue top=5% left=24% rotate=-10 size=1.2em fragment=pop index=1 >}}
{{< bam "PY" colour=yellow top=6% right=8% rotate=8 size=1.2em fragment=pop index=2 >}}
{{< bam "SQL" colour=red bottom=45% left=22% rotate=6 size=1.2em fragment=pop index=3 >}}
{{< boom "TYPST" colour=blue bottom=45% right=6% rotate=-8 size=1.1em fragment=pop index=4 >}}

::: {.caption .fragment data-fragment-index="4"}
The vocabulary is stable.

The question is never "what should the API look like?"  
— only "how do I wire it to a new backend?"
:::

## "How do I wire this grammar to a new rendering backend?" {.speech}

::: {.fragment}
::: {.thought}
Wilkinson solved the hard problem already.
:::

The vocabulary is stable.
The backend is just a backend.
:::

# Issue 03: Gribouille {.section}

A native Typst package.

## The Import {.panel}

```typst
#import "@preview/gribouille:0.2.1": *
```

::: {style="padding-top: 2em;"}
:::

::: {layout-ncol=2}

::: {}
- One line.

- No R session.
:::

::: {}
- No Python environment.

- No recorded libraries.
:::

:::

::: {.caption}
Available on the Typst Universe registry \
— the same channel as every other Typst package.
:::

## data + aes() {.panel}

```{typst}
//| echo: true
//| label: step1
//| width: 14cm
//| height: 9cm
//| alt: Empty plot frame with bill length on the x-axis and bill depth on the y-axis — the coordinate system is established before any layer is added.
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep"),
  layers: (geom-blank(),),
  labs: labs(x: "Bill Length (mm)", y: "Bill Depth (mm)"),
  theme: theme-comic(),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

## + geom-point() {.panel}

```typst
layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt),),
```

```{typst}
//| echo: false
//| label: step2
//| width: 14cm
//| height: 9cm
//| alt: "Scatter plot of bill length vs bill depth for 344 penguins, all marks in a single ink colour on a cream panel with a bold border."
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep"),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt),),
  labs: labs(x: "Bill Length (mm)", y: "Bill Depth (mm)"),
  theme: theme-comic(),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

## + aes(colour = "species") {.panel}

```typst
mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep", fill: "species"),
scales: (scale-fill-discrete(palette: comic-colours),),
```

```{typst}
//| echo: false
//| label: step3
//| width: 14cm
//| height: 9cm
//| alt: "Scatter plot of bill length vs bill depth coloured by species: Adelie in red, Chinstrap in blue, Gentoo in yellow, with a legend."
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep", fill: "species"),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt, colour: black, stroke: 0.5pt),),
  scales: (
    scale-fill-discrete(
      limits: ("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"),
      palette: comic-colours,
    ),
  ),
  labs: labs(x: "Bill Length (mm)", y: "Bill Depth (mm)", fill: "Species"),
  theme: theme-comic(),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

## + facet-wrap("species") {.panel}

```typst
facet: facet-wrap("species"),
```

```{typst}
//| echo: false
//| label: step4
//| width: 14cm
//| height: 9cm
//| alt: "Scatter plot faceted by species into three panels — Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo — each framed with a bold border and a yellow strip header."
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep", fill: "species"),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt, colour: black, stroke: 0.5pt),),
  facet: facet-wrap("species"),
  scales: (
    scale-fill-discrete(
      limits: ("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"),
      palette: comic-colours,
    ),
  ),
  guides: guides(fill: none),
  labs: labs(x: "Bill Length (mm)", y: "Bill Depth (mm)"),
  theme: theme-comic(),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

## compose() {.panel}

```typst
compose(
  defer(plot, ...)),
  defer(plot, ...)),
  columns: 2,
  labs: labs(title: comic-title("Palmer Penguins: Morphometrics")),
  collect: ("fill",),
)
```

```{typst}
//| echo: false
//| label: compose
//| alt: "Two-panel composition: bill length vs bill depth on the left, flipper length vs body mass on the right, with a shared species colour legend."
//| width: 16cm
//| height: 9cm
#let my-plot(x, y, xlab, ylab) = {
  defer(plot,
    data: penguins,
    mapping: aes(x: x, y: y, fill: "species"),
    layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt, colour: black, stroke: 0.5pt),),
    scales: (scale-fill-discrete(limits: ("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"), palette: comic-colours,),),
    labs: labs(x: xlab, y: ylab, fill: "Species"),
    theme: theme-comic()
  )
}
#compose(
  my-plot("bill-len", "bill-dep", "Bill Length (mm)", "Bill Depth (mm)",),
  my-plot("flipper-len", "body-mass", "Flipper Length (mm)", "Body Mass (g)",),
  columns: 2,
  labs: labs(title: comic-title("Palmer Penguins: Morphometrics")),
  collect: ("fill",),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

::: {.caption .fragment}
Contrary to ggplot2/R, Typst does not have lazy evaluation, so we need a way to get the plot specification without immediately rendering it.
:::

## Full Typst Integration {.panel}

```typst
annotate(
  "typst", ...,
  label: [#box(fill: rgb("#fdf6e3"))[#text(size: 24pt)[_Typical_ Chinstrap]]]
),
annotate("vline", xintercept: 45.0, colour: comic-red, stroke: 1pt),
```

```{typst}
//| echo: false
//| label: annotate
//| width: 14cm
//| height: 9cm
//| alt: "Scatter plot with a vertical red reference line at bill length 45 mm and an italic Typst annotation 'typical Chinstrap' placed near the cluster."
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep", fill: "species"),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, colour: black, stroke: 0.5pt),
    annotate("vline", xintercept: 45.0, colour: comic-red, stroke: 1pt),
    annotate(
      "typst",
      x: 35.0,
      y: 14.0,
      label: [#box(fill: rgb("#fdf6e3"))[#text(size: 24pt)[_Typical_ Chinstrap]]],
      anchor: "west",
      size: 8pt,
    ),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(
      limits: ("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"),
      palette: comic-colours,
    ),
  ),
  labs: labs(x: "Bill Length (mm)", y: "Bill Depth (mm)", colour: "Species"),
  theme: theme-comic(),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

::: {.caption .fragment}
The figure is Typst content.

[Annotate, caption, and cross-reference it like any other document element.]{.fragment}
:::

# Issue 04: No Big Dependencies {.section}

Bring a CSV. Nothing else.

## Bring Your Own Data {.panel}

```typst
#let raw = csv("penguins.csv", row-type: dictionary)
#let df = raw
  .filter(r => r.at("bill_len") != "NA")
  .map(r => (
    species:    r.species,
    "bill-len": float(r.at("bill_len")),
    "bill-dep": float(r.at("bill_dep")),
  ))
#plot(data: df, ...)
```

```{typst}
//| echo: false
//| label: csv
//| width: 14cm
//| height: 9cm
//| alt: "Scatter plot of bill length vs bill depth loaded from a CSV file and coloured by species — same result as the built-in penguins dataset."
#let raw = csv("assets/data/penguins.csv", row-type: dictionary)
#let df = (
  raw
  .filter(r => r.at("bill_len") != "NA" and r.at("bill_dep") != "NA")
  .map(r => (
    species:    r.species,
    "bill-len": float(r.at("bill_len")),
    "bill-dep": float(r.at("bill_dep")),
  ))
)
#plot(
  data: df,
  mapping: aes(x: "bill-len", y: "bill-dep", fill: "species"),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt, colour: black, stroke: 0.5pt),),
  scales: (
    scale-fill-discrete(
      limits: ("Adelie", "Chinstrap", "Gentoo"),
      palette: comic-colours,
    ),
  ),
  labs: labs(
    x: "Bill Length (mm)",
    y: "Bill Depth (mm)",
    fill: "Species",
    caption: "Source: penguins.csv",
  ),
  theme: theme-comic(),
  width: auto,
  height: auto,
)
```

::: {.caption .fragment}
No R. No Python. No recorded libraries.
:::

## The Quarto Pipeline {.panel}

::: {.narration}
The same pipeline works in every Quarto format.
:::

| Step           | What happens                                       |
| -------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| Quarto         | Reads `.qmd`, runs pre-Quarto filters.             |
| `typst-render` | Compiles `{typst}` blocks to PNG or SVG.           |
| Gribouille     | Runs inside the Typst compiler — no extra runtime. |
| Output         | HTML, PDF, DOCX — choose your format.              |

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false" title="Bonus"}
The Typst compiler is a single fast binary.  
No package managers, no virtual environments, no version conflicts.
:::

## Typographic Integration {.panel}

In a native Typst document, `typst-render` injects the document's foreground and background colours into every compiled block.

Figure axis text inherits the document font at document size.  
The figure and the prose are compiled by the same engine.

::: {.callout-note collapse="false" title="In practice"}
Switch your document font.  
Switch your document colour scheme.  
Your figures follow automatically.
:::

::: {.caption}
This is the integration gap that an SVG import can never close.
:::

# Issue 05: Origin Story {.section}

The extension came first.

## The First Piece {.panel}

**`quarto-typst-render`** came before Gribouille.

It proved that a `{typst}` code block inside a Quarto document could compile to an image and land in any output format.

That proof of concept opened the question: what if the figures *were* Typst, not just Typst-compiled images of something else?

::: {.caption}
Gribouille is the answer.
`quarto-typst-render` is the delivery mechanism.
:::

## Timeline {.panel}

::: {.narration}
One month planning/investigating.  
One month coding (*ground initial work*).  
Followed by ~200 agentic sessions.
:::

::: {.columns style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
::: {.column}

**March 2026: Planning**

- Grammar design decisions.
- Theme API specification.
- Geom and stat priority list.
- `cetz` as the drawing layer.

:::
::: {.column}

**May 2026: Shipping**

- LLM-assisted architecture.
- ~200 agentic coding sessions.
- Human review at every commit.
- v0.1.0 released: May 20.
- v0.2.0 released: June 1.
- v0.3.0 released: June 24.

:::
:::

{{< boom "Really!" colour=red bottom=20% right=2% rotate=-8 size=1.4em fragment=burst >}}
{{< bam "v0.3.0" colour=yellow bottom=0% right=-2% rotate=10 size=1.3em fragment=pop >}}

## Under the Hood {.panel}

**`cetz`** provides the low-level drawing primitives:
lines, curves, shapes, coordinate transforms.

**Gribouille** is the grammar layer on top:
scales, stats, geoms, guides, themes, are all in pure Typst.

No Rust extensions.
No JavaScript.
No native binaries beyond the Typst compiler itself (_for now_).

# Issue 06: Current State {.section}

{{< boom "v0.3.0" colour=red top=60% right=18% rotate=-8 size=1.6em fragment=burst >}}

## What Ships {.panel}

::: {layout-ncol=2}

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="1"}
**Geoms**

`geom-point`, `geom-line`, `geom-bar`, `geom-col`, `geom-area`, `geom-boxplot`, `geom-violin`, `geom-histogram`, `geom-contour`, `geom-hex`, `geom-tile`, `geom-text`, `geom-smooth`, `geom-rug` ...
:::

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="2"}
**Scales**

continuous, discrete, manual; colour (brewer, viridis, okabe-ito, gradient); size, alpha, shape, linetype ...
:::

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="3"}
**Themes**

`theme-grey`, `theme-minimal`, `theme-classic`, `theme-bw`, `theme-void`, `theme-linedraw`, `theme-light`, `theme-dark`
:::

::: {.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="4"}
**More**

Facets, `compose()`/`defer()`, coords, full accessibility guide, `labs()`, `guides()`, `annotate()`, stats ...
:::

:::

{{< bam "20+" colour=red top=2% left=20% rotate=-8 size=1.5em fragment=pop index=1 >}}
{{< bam "30+" colour=blue top=2% right=10% rotate=6 size=1.5em fragment=pop index=2 >}}
{{< boom "8" colour=yellow bottom=-25% left=38% rotate=-6 fragment=pop index=3 >}}

## Honest Limitations {.panel}

::: {.callout-warning collapse="false" title="What is not there (_yet_)"}

- **Static output only.**  
  Typst is a document compiler.
- **Performance at scale.**  
  Plots with thousands of points increase Typst compile time.  
  (_not benchmarked yet_)
- **Not all ggplot2 extensions are ported.**  
  "maps" and complex stat pipelines are still missing.  
  (_WASM modules are possible, but not yet implemented_)

:::

## Where to Help {.panel}

Feedbacks are welcome:

- [Bug reports — the test suite is young; edge cases are still being found.]{.fragment .comic-pop}
- [Documentation examples — worked/real examples in different domains.]{.fragment .comic-pop}

::: {.caption .fragment}
Gribouille is an unfunded spare-time project, and some parts of the API is still settling.  
[Pull requests are not being accepted for now.]{.fragment style="text-decoration: underline;"}
:::

# Issue 07: Closing {.section}

To be continued...

## Links {.panel}

::: {.narration}
Gribouille is a scribble today.  
The grammar is stable enough to build on.
:::

- [**Typst Universe** — `@preview/gribouille:0.2.1`]{.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="1"}
- [**Docs** — [`m.canouil.dev/gribouille`](https://m.canouil.dev/gribouille)]{.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="2"}
- [**GitHub** — [`github.com/mcanouil/gribouille`](https://github.com/mcanouil/gribouille)]{.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="3"}
- [**This talk** — [`mcanouil.github.io/2026-ggplot2-extenders-gribouille`](https://mcanouil.github.io/2026-ggplot2-extenders-gribouille)]{.fragment .comic-pop data-fragment-index="4"}
- [**TidyTuesday** - [`m.canouil.dev/tidytuesday`](https://m.canouil.dev/tidytuesday/)]

{{< bam "v0.3.0" colour=yellow bottom=-15% right=25% rotate=12 size=2em fragment=burst index=4 >}}

::: {.caption .fragment data-fragment-index="5"}
Thank you.
:::
